text:
{
  "title": "Response Completeness Signature",
  "group_name": "response_completeness_signature",
  "family": "informative_missingness",
  "summary": "Represent each student's pattern of unanswered or unavailable health and lifestyle fields as a behavioral and measurement-completeness signal rather than treating missing values only as preprocessing noise.",
  "depends_on": [],
  "strategy": "Before any imputation, create binary missing indicators for each original non-id feature using is-null plus blank-string checks for categorical fields; add row-level missing_count and missing_ratio over all 13 features; add missing counts and ratios for numeric measurement fields versus categorical self-report fields; add focused counts for wellness-report fields such as stress, sleep, activity, smoking/alcohol, diet, and gender; add a compact completeness_bin with values complete, low_missing for 1-2 missing fields, moderate_missing for 3-4, and high_missing for 5 or more; if a column is absent in a future split, omit only that column's indicator and compute denominators from the available expected columns.",
  "expected_signal": "Missingness can be non-random in health and survey data, and the provided columns have substantial missing rates, so co-missingness across stress, sleep, activity, smoking/alcohol, and sensor-like measurements may identify risk strata that raw observed values alone do not separate, especially for balanced accuracy on smaller classes; background sources consulted include https://feat.engineering/08-Handling_Missing_Data.html, https://arxiv.org/abs/2202.10580, and https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00127-011-0387-3.",
  "risk": "The signal may overfit collection or generation artifacts if train and test missingness mechanisms differ, and it may be partly redundant with downstream handling of NaN values; per-feature indicators also add moderate dimensionality without adding physiological content."
}
